question_text:
Hi, I just transferred to the Zurich branch this morning and my setup is completely broken.
First, when I dial external vendor numbers on the desktop app, the line is dead silent—I can't hear them at all, though they say they hear me.
Second, I'm locked out of the 'ERP_Upgrade' directory on the intranet; it just says I don't have permission, which makes no sense since I viewed it yesterday.
Finally, I tried to send a scanned contract from the hallway printer to a vendor's email. The printer screen showed a green checkmark, but the vendor never received the email. Please fix my account!
answer: Recommendation: second_level_support What the indexed records explicitly document (evidence) - One‑way / silent external audio that affects users at a site has in the KB been caused by site edge / firewall / Direct‑Routing media restrictions and required UC/Network engineering to fix (Zurich outbound firewall change blocked RTP; engineers applied blueprint‑aligned firewall/SBC changes) [Data: Sources (5)]. - A single‑user audio symptom that was resolved by first‑line helpdesk is documented (microphone cable reseat after a Teams Test Call), so purely local hardware faults have precedent for L1 resolution - but that was an isolated single‑user hardware case (TI‑1616128) [Data: Sources (37)]. - Intranet/folder access denials after a transfer or change have in the KB been caused by token/Conditional‑Access timing or by AD/Azure AD group/object synchronization/nesting issues; those investigations used Entra/Azure AD membership and sign‑in logs and in several cases required identity/BU‑Infrastructure intervention [Data: Sources (38); Reports (13)]. - Scan‑to‑Mail cases where the MFP showed a local "success" while the external recipient received nothing were repeatedly diagnosed to the SMTP relay (either "MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS FIXED LIMIT" for oversized scans, or "Relay denied" where a new printer VLAN IP range was not whitelisted). Remediations in the KB involved Mail/Relay and Network changes (whitelisting VLANs, adjusting relay limits) and then validating delivery [Data: Reports (36); Reports (6)]. - Multi‑domain incidents (voice + directory + printing/mail) in the corpus were routinely escalated for coordinated engineer action rather than closed by first‑line alone (examples and post‑mortems reference escalation to infrastructure/engineering for cross‑system faults) [Data: Reports (16); Sources (28)]. Why I recommend second_level_support (concise reasoning) - Your ticket reports three concurrent faults spanning distinct systems: telephony/media (Teams/desktop app), identity/permissions (ERP_Upgrade folder), and mail/printing (MFP -> external vendor). The indexed KB shows that: (a) site‑wide/edge telephony media faults were handled by UC/Network engineers (not L1) [Data: Sources (5)]; (b) folder access regressions sometimes needed Identity team/AD/Azure AD sync analysis (not always a simple gpupdate) [Data: Sources (38)]; and (c) scan‑to‑mail failures where the device UI lied required Mail/Relay and Network coordination to whitelist VLANs or change relay policy [Data: Reports (36); Reports (6)]. Together, this combination historically required cross‑team engineer involvement to diagnose and remediate, so classify as second_level_support. - Note: if investigation shows the telephony symptom is a single‑endpoint hardware problem (like TI‑1616128) and the other two problems are independent first‑line fixes, L1 could close-but the simultaneous, multi‑domain nature of your report matches prior cases that were escalated [Data: Sources (37); Sources (5); Reports (36)]. Recommended immediate actions (these are recommendations, not claims of prior actions in the KB) - Triage concurrently and collect artifacts for escalation: Teams Test Call result + client/version and timestamp; SIP/SBC traces or call attempt timestamps (for UC/Network); Azure AD/Entra membership export for the user (direct + nested groups) and recent sign‑in logs around the transfer time; printer job timestamp/ID, printer IP/VLAN and SMTP relay logs for that timestamp. (Recommended - consistent with diagnostics used in the cited incidents but not claimed as already performed here.) - If the Teams Test Call proves the endpoint audio is functioning locally, escalate UC/Network immediately (site‑edge media/firewall or SBC). If the SMTP relay shows "Relay denied" or size rejects, involve Mail/Network to whitelist the printer VLAN or adjust relay policy. If Azure AD membership/sync looks inconsistent, involve Identity/BU‑Infrastructure. (Recommended next‑owners based on past-case roles.) Key supporting records cited - Zurich one‑way audio caused by firewall change (escalated to UC/Network) [Data: Sources (5)]. - Single‑user microphone reseat resolved at first‑line (example where L1 closed one‑way audio) [Data: Sources (37)]. - ERP_Upgrade / token/Conditional Access and identity‑plane diagnostics (use of Azure AD sign‑in logs and membership checks in prior cases) [Data: Sources (38); Reports (13)]. - Scan‑to‑Mail: "MESSAGE SIZE EXCEEDS FIXED LIMIT" and "Relay denied" findings; remediation by Mail/Relay and VLAN whitelisting [Data: Reports (36); Reports (6)]. - Multi‑domain incidents that required infrastructure/engineering coordination (precedent for escalation) [Data: Reports (16); Sources (28)]. If you want, I can now draft the exact minimal artifact list and the short wording to include in the escalation ticket so the UC/Network, Identity and Mail teams receive the logs and timestamps they need (this would be a recommended action, not a KB excerpt).

question_text: URGENT MAJOR INCIDENT: We are getting flooded with calls from the Sales and Finance teams after the HR department updated the organizational chart in the system last night. First, our freelance consultants are complaining they are locked out of their shared workspaces and get an error screen. Second, the sales reps are trying to call clients, but the dialer just beeps and drops the call immediately. Finally, the finance team in Zurich is furious because their confidential invoices are physically printing out in the Basel office. What on earth did the org chart update break?
answer: Recommendation - Classify this ticket as: second_level_support. What the indexed records explicitly document (evidence) - Cross‑system failures that follow an HR/org‑chart or AD/group change (guest/workspace access loss, telephony/dialer failures, and site‑scoped printer mapping) were handled as engineering escalations in prior incidents. Examples of the relevant prior cases: - External/guest access loss caused by a cloud vs on‑prem group/object collision and fixed by group consolidation + full Azure AD Connect sync [Data: Sources (13)]. - Sales outbound dialing failing because a provisioning/script or voice‑routing‑policy assignment was incorrect and required policy/provisioning fixes (engineer involvement) [Data: Sources (9)]. - Finance users receiving the wrong site printers after a Finance-group nesting error; group membership/nesting fixes were applied (ticketed remediation) [Data: Sources (2)]. - SBC/Direct‑Routing incidents where missing translation entries or session‑timer/NAT mismatches required SBC/config and carrier coordination to resolve (temporary translation rule -> permanent update; session‑timer changes) [Data: Sources (8); Sources (16)]. Why this ticket should be second_level_support (brief reasoning) - The reported symptoms span identity/provisioning (freelancers locked out of shared workspaces), Unified Communications/Direct Routing (dialer beeps and immediate drop), and site/GPO/print mapping (invoices printing at the wrong office). In the indexed knowledge base, the same pattern - simultaneous, reproducible failures across identity, telephony, and print routing after an org/group change - repeatedly required specialist investigation and hands‑on engineering changes (Azure AD Connect/group consolidation, voice routing policy or SBC config edits, GPO/printer membership corrections). Those prior tickets were not resolved by routine first‑line runbook steps but by second‑level teams (identity/infra, UC/SBC engineers, Core Engineering) [Data: Sources (13); Sources (9); Sources (2); Sources (8); Sources (16)]. What the records do not (and so cannot) tell us about this specific incident - The indexed records do not prove which exact AD/Azure‑AD objects changed, which actor performed the HR update, or the exact timestamps for the change in your environment. They document the failure modes and how similar events were remediated, but not the raw logs from your tenant or the live change audit for this event [Data: Sources (13); Sources (2)]. Immediate next steps (recommended, not purely record‑extracted) - Escalate to identity (Azure AD/Entra) engineering, Unified Communications/SBC owners, and print/GPO owners for coordinated investigation (recommended). Collect the change timestamp and AD/Azure AD audit entries, Teams/Direct Routing/SBC call traces and correlation IDs for failed calls, and the print server/queue mapping and recent group‑membership deltas (recommended). These are recommended actions consistent with triage patterns used in the past but are not themselves verbatim actions recorded for this specific ticket in the indexed corpus. Key supporting references (selected) - Guest/cloud vs on‑prem group collision -> remediation with consolidation + full sync: TI‑1583327 [Data: Sources (13)]. - Sales outbound dialing / provisioning voice‑policy misassignment -> required policy/provisioning fix: TI‑1609823 [Data: Sources (9)]. - Finance group nesting causing Zurich printers to apply to Basel users -> group membership/nesting correction: TI‑1623017 [Data: Sources (2)]. - SBC translation table missing new number range (INVITE -> 404) and temporary rule -> permanent update: TI‑1612036 [Data: Sources (8)]. - Session‑timer / NAT timeout causing short call teardowns (BYE ~29-31s) -> SBC/NAT/session‑timer changes: TI‑1604879 / related Direct Routing incidents [Data: Sources (16)]. If you want, I can draft the minimal escalation note (one paragraph) to paste into the ticket that lists the exact artifacts to attach (change timestamp, Azure AD audit IDs/ObjectIDs, sample Teams call timestamps + Admin Center correlation IDs, and print‑server queue logs). That draft would be a recommended artifact to speed a second‑level handoff.

question_text:
Hi IT, our compliance audit team flagged something on one of our project folders a couple of weeks ago and asked us to make some changes to it. We did the changes — at least we thought we did — and now things have been weird ever since, in confusing ways for different groups of people.

The most frustrating part is who is affected. Some of the external partners we work with on the project tell us they can sometimes open the folder and see the files, and then later in the same day they cannot. They say nothing has changed on their side — same browser, same network, same password as always. Then on our side, two of our regular team members say they can see the folder name in their file list but when they click any document inside, it just will not open or they get blocked. The files used to open fine for them just last month.

We have a specific user in finance who said her access keeps flickering: she had access in the morning, lost it around lunch, got it back briefly after restarting her laptop, then lost it again. Her manager already checked everything and her account looks completely normal.

And separately — though we cannot tell if it is connected — a totally different folder used by a different team has stopped appearing in their file list at all. They can still get to it if they have the URL bookmarked, but it is like the folder has gone invisible for some of them.

People are blaming us, the audit team, the project lead, and even the IT contractor who did some work last month. Everyone has a theory. What is actually going on, and how do we get this under control before someone blames the wrong people?
answer: ## Recommendation - Classification second_level_support ## Short rationale (concise) The ticket's symptom set - folder rename/changes followed by: - external partners intermittently able to open files then later denied, - internal users seeing folders but failing to open documents, - access temporarily restored after client restart or re‑authentication, - and other folders becoming "invisible" except by direct URL - matches multiple prior incidents that required identity/SharePoint engineering (not routine first‑line fixes). Those past cases were resolved only after engineer‑level work (Entra/Azure AD group/object reconciliation, sensitivity‑label reapplication or group alignment, full Azure AD Connect syncs, and token invalidation) rather than by standard first‑line scripts. This classification and reasoning follow the documented incident set for this tenant [Data: Sources (30); Sources (13); Sources (38)]. ## Evidence from the indexed records (what the records explicitly document) - A documented Sensitivity‑Label vs ACL divergence where a label was applied after unique folder permissions (and "Preserve custom permissions" was not enabled) produced exactly this intermittent behavior; remediation required label/group alignment and token invalidation [Data: Sources (30)]. - A documented Azure AD Connect / same‑display‑name group collision (cloud vs on‑prem group with different ObjectIDs) caused external guests to be excluded; remediation required group consolidation and a full Azure AD Connect sync [Data: Sources (13)]. - Token‑caching / Conditional Access timing produced "works temporarily then fails" symptoms in another recorded case; forcing full sign‑out (Office + browser) and re‑authentication was used as an immediate mitigation during investigations [Data: Sources (38)]. (Each of the three failure modes above - label↔ACL mismatch, group/object‑ID collision, and token caching/CA timing - appears in the prior records as a root cause for the same mixed symptoms you describe [Data: Sources (30); Sources (13); Sources (38)].) ## Why this is second_level_support (brief) - The prior tickets that match these symptoms (sensitivity‑label interactions, AAD group/object collisions, token timing effects) were escalated to BU Infrastructure / M365 / Identity and required system‑engineer actions (objectID reconciliation, group-nesting fixes, full AD Connect syncs, label reapply and token invalidation) - i.e., engineer involvement was necessary to fix and to prevent recurrence [Data: Sources (30); Sources (13); Sources (38)]. ## Immediate next steps (recommended - these are actions suggested based on the indexed cases, not additional documented facts) (Please treat these as recommended triage actions to perform now and/or hand to identity engineers.) 1. Ask impacted users to fully sign out of Office desktop apps and browsers (or revoke refresh tokens for a test user) and re‑test - prior incidents used this as an immediate mitigation to force token refresh and to confirm token‑caching effects [Data: Sources (38)]. 2. Capture evidence for escalation: SharePoint folder permission export (which principal/ObjectID the ACL references), the folder's Purview/AIP label and its allowed principal(s), Azure AD sign‑in logs for representative affected users (timestamps of successful vs failed access and token claims), and Azure AD group objectIDs and membership listings for groups referenced by the ACL and the label. These are the exact artifacts used in past investigations [Data: Sources (30); Sources (13); Sources (38)]. 3. If the above evidence shows a label↔ACL divergence or group ObjectID mismatch, escalate to your identity/M365 engineering team (second level) to reconcile group objects, reapply or reconfigure the label as needed, and (if required) run a controlled full Azure AD Connect sync and token invalidation - those were the remediation steps recorded as effective in the prior cases [Data: Sources (30); Sources (13)]. If you'd like, I can draft the minimal evidence list you should collect now (exact log fields and the specific items to export) and a short escalation brief referencing the prior tickets so identity engineers get the context and artifacts they need. (That drafting would be a recommendation based on the documented investigations, not a new record of actions performed.)

question_text: Hi IT, we've had a strange situation develop over the past two weeks where multiple unrelated-looking issues have surfaced across different parts of the organization. We cannot tell if these are connected to one underlying change or multiple separate things. First, when people try to open our internal HR portal or other internal-only websites in their browsers, they get a big warning page about not being able to trust the connection. They have to manually click through to bypass it. Internal websites only — public sites work fine. Second, our home-working population (about 80 people) say their secure work-from-home connection refuses to authenticate. The connection software says it cannot verify the server identity. They were working fine until a couple of weeks ago, and nothing changed on their laptops. Third, our office wireless network has been showing as unavailable for newer team members and people who recently got replacement laptops — the network just does not show up in their list of available networks at all. Their laptops are correctly registered in our standard management system. Fourth, when people try to set up their work email on iPhones — particularly people who switched phones recently — the setup goes around in a loop. The email app starts, then shows a message about device approval, then drops back to the start. The context we have: our infrastructure team did some kind of internal maintenance about three weeks ago, and our security team tightened some internal rules around the same time. We cannot tell if these are causing all four problems, two separate causes, or whether something else is going on. We need an analysis.
answer: Classification: second_level_support What the indexed records explicitly document (evidence from past incidents) - Internal-only browser TLS trust warnings like your HR portal symptom were recorded in TI-1700005; root cause there was a renewed issuing/intermediate CA not being published to the internal AIA distribution point. Remediation recorded: publish the intermediate to AIA/CRL and force client refresh (gpupdate / certutil -pulse) which cleared client warnings [Data: Sources (55)]. - VPN authentication failures where clients "could not verify the server identity" match TI-1700006: CheckPoint IKEv2 logs showed an EKU mismatch because the post‑renewal VPN server certificate was issued from a template that omitted the required IPsec/IKE EKU. Remediation recorded: fix the template, re‑issue the server cert, deploy to the concentrator and restart - affected users recovered [Data: Sources (104)]. - Office Wi‑Fi missing on new/replacement devices was documented as an Intune/profile scoping issue in prior cases: Wi‑Fi profiles assigned to a compliance‑based dynamic group were "not applicable" for devices flagged non‑compliant (e.g., after a tightened BitLocker escrow policy); resolving actions included changing assignment scope so new devices received the profile [Data: Sources (87)]. - Outlook on iOS setup loops tied to device-approval were recorded where App‑Protection/MAM required device compliance; affected iPhones were non‑compliant under a tightened device evaluation policy and onboarding looped until policy rescoping or remediation was applied (TI-1700011) [Data: Sources (20)]. - Related incidents show SCEP/NDES and on‑prem PKI dependencies and connector issues were part of the same maintenance / PKI-change impact set; NDES connector logs and connector reinstall actions were recorded as part of other remediation efforts [Data: Sources (93)]. Why this supports second_level_support (brief reasoning) - The ticket's combination of (a) internal TLS chain trust failures, (b) VPN server‑identity/EKU failures, (c) Intune/profile delivery issues for new devices, and (d) mobile App‑Protection onboarding loops maps directly to multiple prior incidents that required PKI, VPN/network, Endpoint/Intune and Mobile/App‑Protection engineering actions - not routine helpdesk steps. The indexed cases above were handled by infrastructure/PKI and endpoint teams and involved certificate/template fixes, CA/AIA publication, re‑issuance/deployment of server certs, and Intune policy/assignment changes [Data: Sources (55, 104, 87, 20, 93)]. - Because those prior tickets required cross‑team engineering coordination (PKI + Network/VPN + Endpoint + Mobile), the same symptoms in this ticket indicate it should be escalated to second_level_support. Recommended immediate triage (these are recommendations, not verbatim facts from the tickets) - Collect representative artifacts used successfully in the past: browser certificate‑chain screenshots and certutil -urlfetch -verify outputs for affected internal sites; VPN/CheckPoint IKE logs showing EKU/chain errors; per‑device Intune compliance‑evaluation history and Wi‑Fi/profile assignment applicability; iOS device enrollment/App‑Protection failure traces; and NDES connector logs if SCEP is in use. These are the same classes of diagnostics the prior incidents used to reach root cause [Recommended; based on practices recorded in the tickets] [Data: Sources (55, 104, 87, 20, 93)]. - Escalate to PKI (issuing CA / AIA/CRL), Network/VPN (CheckPoint), Endpoint/Intune and Mobile/App‑Protection owners for coordinated triage (documented owner roles in prior cases). Primary source records relied on - TI-1700005 (HR portal TLS chain / missing AIA publish) [Data: Sources (55)]. - TI-1700006 (CheckPoint VPN IKEv2 EKU mismatch and remediation) [Data: Sources (104)]. - Intune / Wi‑Fi / profile assignment incidents (replacement laptops / compliance‑scoping) [Data: Sources (87)]. - Outlook-on‑iOS / App‑Protection setup‑loop incident (device approval loop) [Data: Sources (20)]. - NDES/NDES‑connector related log/remediation examples (connector logs, reinstall) [Data: Sources (93)]. If you want, I can (as a recommended next step) produce a short list of the exact artifact types and the single representative devices/accounts to collect first so the engineering teams can begin evidence review immediately. (Marked recommended - not claimed as present in the indexed records.)

question_text:
Hi IT, our team did some kind of internal infrastructure maintenance over a weekend a couple of weeks ago — something behind the scenes that wasn't supposed to affect anyone visibly. Since then we have been getting a series of reports from different parts of the company that look unrelated but all started around the same time.

First, when people try to open our internal HR system in their browsers — to look at their records or report sick days — their browsers throw a big warning page saying they cannot trust the connection. They have to click through some 'advanced' option to actually get to the page. Same thing on a couple of other internal websites that everyone normally uses.

Second, our remote workers who work from home or while travelling — about 80 people — say they cannot get their secure work-from-home connection established anymore. Their connection software just refuses to authenticate. The error mentions something about not being able to verify the server identity. They were working fine until this maintenance work happened, and nothing changed on their laptops.

Third, the leadership team uses a special configuration for their email correspondence so that confidential communications can be cryptographically verified — outgoing messages they send carry a kind of seal, and they can read sealed messages they receive from external partners. That whole feature stopped working. They cannot apply the seal to outgoing email anymore, and they cannot read sealed email they receive. They were given new credentials around the time of the maintenance work, but those credentials do not seem to do anything.

Fourth, several of our internal business applications — the HR app, our SharePoint installation, and our network access control system — report that they can no longer reach our central user-account system through the protected channel they normally use. They CAN reach it through the unprotected channel, but our security policy does not allow operating that way.

These all started in the same window. Different teams are pointing at each other — the infrastructure team that did the maintenance, the application owners, the security team. We need an actual answer about what is happening.
answer: ## Classification (recommended) second_level_support ## Short rationale The symptom set in this ticket - simultaneous browser "untrusted connection" warnings for multiple internal HTTPS sites, VPN clients failing with "cannot verify server identity", executive S/MIME signing/reading failures after credential/credential‑provisioning activity, and multiple internal apps unable to use the protected (LDAPS/TLS) channel to the account system - matches prior incidents that were root‑caused to PKI / certificate/template / CA‑publishing problems and that required PKI, network and identity engineers to fix. Those past incidents required template/CA changes, certificate re‑issuance or AIA publishing, DC re‑enrollment, and coordinated cross‑team remediation rather than routine front‑line steps, so this should be handled by second‑level engineering (PKI + Identity + Network) [Data: Reports (13); Sources (104); Reports (112)]. ## What the indexed records explicitly document (most relevant parallels) - Missing/incorrect CA publication or template changes produced browser certificate‑chain trust failures and required PKI owner intervention to republish the issuing CA / fix AIA distribution and re‑deploy certs [Data: Reports (13)]. - A certificate‑template change that omitted required EKUs caused large numbers of remote VPN users to fail mutual‑auth (IKEv2) and required certificate/template correction and re‑issuance [Data: Sources (104)]. - Executive S/MIME (sign/verify) stopped working when private‑key delivery/archival or certificate provisioning failed during a rollout; resolution required PKI key‑recovery and secure delivery of PFXs under controlled procedures [Data: Reports (112)]. - Applications (e.g., domain controllers, LDAPS consumers) lost secure‑channel connectivity where the recent certificate/template changes produced incompatible DC certificates - remediation included template rollback/reissue and DC re‑enrollment [Data: Reports (13)]. These documented incident types map directly to the four concurrent failure modes you describe (browser TLS trust, VPN server identity verification, S/MIME signing/reading, and LDAPS/protected‑channel failures) and in prior cases required specialist engineering escalation and cross‑team fixes [Data: Reports (13); Sources (104); Reports (112)]. ## Why this is not first_level_support - The combination of PKI/CA, VPN mutual‑auth, S/MIME private‑key delivery, and LDAPS failures points at certificate lifecycle/template/AIA issues (or similar root causes) rather than routine user/client resets or single‑app configuration errors. In the indexed incidents with the same constellation of failures, front‑line steps (clear browser cache, restart, re‑enroll device) were insufficient; engineer actions (fix CA/template, re‑publish AIA, re‑issue certificates, perform key recovery) were required and recorded [Data: Reports (13); Sources (104); Reports (112)]. ## Recommended next steps (these are recommendations - NOT direct excerpts of prior tickets) Label these as "immediate evidence to collect and hand to PKI/Identity/Network engineers": - Capture an HTTPS chain for one affected internal HR site: browser screenshot of the warning, server certificate chain (export DER/PEM), and server hostname/FQDN and timestamp. - Collect VPN client logs from several affected remote users and VPN concentrator logs showing the server‑identity / certificate verification failure (include timestamps and sample client IPs). - For leadership S/MIME cases: list affected leader UPNs, sample signed/received message headers showing failure, and note whether new credentials were issued and when; also ask PKI whether key archive/escrow shows the expected private keys. - From the failing applications (HR app / SharePoint / NAC): export the application TLS/LDAPS error messages and the server‑side certificate presented by the account system (thumbprint, subject, EKU, validity dates). - Ask PKI owners to confirm whether the issuing CA and intermediate certificates are published to the internal AIA/CRL endpoints and whether any certificate templates or EKUs were changed around the weekend maintenance window. - Provide the exact maintenance window date(s) and any change‑ticket ids so engineers can correlate with CA/template changes and AAD/AD CS activity logs. (These evidence items reflect what prior incidents used to diagnose and prove CA/template/AIA failures; the above collection list is a recommended immediate triage package - not a verbatim runbook item in the indexed tickets.) ## Who should own / be engaged Escalate to second_level_support (identity/PKI/network): engage PKI/CA owners, the Identity/AAD team, and Network/VPN engineers simultaneously. Prior incidents required coordinated actions across these teams (PKI to fix templates/publish AIA and reissue, Identity to re‑enroll domain/AD certificates or reconcile cert usage, Network to validate VPN server cert chain) [Data: Reports (13); Sources (104); Reports (112)]. ## Supporting citations (selected) - PKI / CA publish and DC / LDAPS / app TLS failures required CA/template fixes and re‑issuance in prior incidents [Data: Reports (13)]. - VPN IKEv2 mutual‑auth outages following a template/EKU change were fixed by correcting the template and re‑deploying certs in prior cases [Data: Sources (104)]. - Executive S/MIME private‑key provisioning failures were remedied via PKI key‑recovery and secure PFX delivery in prior cases [Data: Reports (112)]. --- If you want, I can now draft a concise evidence‑request message you can send to the application, VPN and PKI owners (a template listing the exact logs/certs/timestamps to provide). That text would be a recommended operational next step and is not itself drawn verbatim from the indexed incident logs - say "draft evidence request" and I will prepare it.

question_text:
Hi IT, we are a couple of weeks into our migration from our old internal sign-in system to the new cloud-based one. The first batch of users went through smoothly, but since we did the second batch about two weeks ago, we have been getting a series of strange complaints from those users that we cannot make sense of.

First, several users from the second batch say they can sign in to their cloud email and Teams without any problem, but when they try to open our internal procurement system or our document management system (which run on our internal servers), they get an error saying their account is not recognized. The first-batch users do not have this issue.

Second, we are seeing strange inconsistencies with security groups. A user might be correctly in the new combined group on the internal directory side, but in the cloud they still show as a member of an old group. This is breaking some access permissions because the cloud and internal systems read different group memberships for the same person.

Third, the same affected users are saying their mobile email apps (built-in iPhone Mail and the Android Gmail app) keep asking for password and never connect. Outlook on the phone still works fine for them but the built-in mail apps just loop on the password prompt.

Fourth — and this only came up because someone noticed overnight reports were missing — several scheduled tasks that run on our internal servers using service accounts have started failing with authentication errors. The service accounts have not been touched by the migration as far as we know, but the failures started right around the time the second batch was migrated.

These all started in the same window but they look like four completely different problems. We need to figure out what is going on and how to fix it.
answer: Recommendation: second_level_support Reasoning (concise) - The four symptom classes in your ticket - (1) cloud sign-in OK while on‑prem/internal apps report "account not recognized", (2) on‑prem vs cloud group membership divergence, (3) built‑in mobile mail apps looping on passwords while Outlook Mobile works, and (4) scheduled tasks using service accounts failing with auth errors - map directly to multiple documented hybrid‑identity migration incidents that required identity/AAD‑Connect and Conditional Access engineering rather than routine helpdesk fixes. This mapping is recorded in the indexed incident corpus. [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Reports (5); Sources (4)] What the records explicitly document (relevant examples) - UPN / provisioning template overwrite during a second migration wave produced exactly the "cloud sign‑in OK but on‑prem app failing" symptom; the recorded remediation was: revert on‑prem userPrincipalName values and run delta -> full Azure AD Connect sync. This was handled by identity engineers (TI‑1700001). [Data: Sources (22)] - AAD Connect OU‑filter omissions (Migration_Phase2 OU excluded) produced group membership divergence (cloud still showing old groups while on‑prem showed new groups). Remediation required updating the OU filter and running delta/full syncs (TI‑1700002). [Data: Sources (53)] - Built‑in mobile mail clients were documented repeatedly to fail when they attempted legacy Basic EAS after tenant modern‑auth enforcement; Outlook Mobile (OAuth) worked and engineers resolved affected phones by re‑adding accounts/forcing OAuth or re‑enrolling in Intune in prior KF70 cases. [Data: Sources (4)] - Scheduled tasks run by service accounts began failing after those accounts were inadvertently synced to the cloud and evaluated by a baseline Conditional Access policy; recorded remediation required correcting AAD Connect scope and creating an exclusion group (KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA) so non‑interactive service accounts would not be blocked - this was executed by identity engineers (TI‑1700004). [Data: Reports (5)] Why this should be escalated - The prior incidents above were not resolved by routine front‑line steps; they required Azure AD Connect configuration inspection/change, full syncs, UPN reversion, Conditional Access policy edits (and creation/population of a service‑account exclusion group), and coordination between identity, app owners and endpoint teams. Those are engineer‑level actions and match the pattern and remediation ownership in the cited tickets. [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Reports (5)] Recommended next actions (labelled as recommendations - not asserted as previously performed) - Escalate to BU Infrastructure - Identity / AAD‑Connect engineering and collect: representative affected UPNs, Azure AD Connect sync run history and current OU filter, cloud vs on‑prem group ObjectIDs and memberships, Conditional Access policy change history, and Task Scheduler logs for failing jobs. (This recommendation follows the investigative artifacts used in the cited incidents; the indexed records show teams requested and used these artifacts during remediation.) [Data: Sources (22); Sources (53); Reports (5)] - Treat the ticket as a multi‑team investigation (Identity + App owners + Endpoint/Intune + Security/CA). (Recommended - consistent with cross‑team remediation recorded in the incidents.) [Data: Reports (5); Sources (4)] Primary supporting records referenced - TI‑1700001 - UPN suffix / on‑prem sign‑in failures; remediation: revert on‑prem UPNs + full AAD Connect sync [Data: Sources (22)]. - TI‑1700002 - Migration_Phase2 OU excluded from AAD Connect -> group sync divergence; remediation: update OU filter, delta/full sync [Data: Sources (53)]. - TI‑1700004 / KF70 scheduled‑task outages - service accounts synced -> CA evaluated -> created KF70‑SERVICEACCOUNTS‑NOMFA exclusion and corrected AAD Connect scope (identity engineer involvement) [Data: Reports (5)]. - KF70 mobile client incidents - built‑in iOS/Android Mail (EAS/BasicAuth) looping; Outlook Mobile/OAuth worked; device re‑add or Intune re‑enroll used as remediation in prior cases [Data: Sources (4)].
